reason: Learning without being warned of a later test is by definition incidental learning.
Retrieval in any order with no cues is free recall.
Being told a test is coming and deliberately retrieving the studied list is an explicit memory test; recognition is a specific format not stated.
Storage is the retention stage occupying the interval between presentation and test; interpretation/committing describe encoding.
To isolate storage you manipulate the retention interval (what happens after presentation, before test); manipulating presentation targets encoding.
Dot probe is an attentional-bias task, not a memory retrieval measure.
Serial position curve: middle items lack both primacy and recency benefit.
Enhanced recall of end-of-list items on immediate test is the recency effect.
A filled/longer retention interval eliminates recency; preventing rehearsal chiefly harms primacy.
Atkinson and Shiffrin (1968) multi-store = the modal model.
Neuropsychological cases (e.g. KF) with impaired STM but intact LTM contradict the modal model's serial STM->LTM route.
Craik & Lockhart: semantic processing is the deepest level.
The classic circularity criticism: no independent way to determine depth of processing except by the memory it produces.
Cues work through overlap with what was encoded (encoding specificity); the other options describe forgetting or capacity, not cue benefit.
Direct quote from Tulving & Thomson's encoding specificity principle.
Retroactive inhibition = new learning interfering with old = an interference account.
Old learning impeding new learning is proactive inhibition/interference.
A and B are near-duplicates; A adds the similarity restriction on proactive interference, which is the more specific and standard claim.
Jost's law is the mathematical statement about relative forgetting rates underlying the forgetting curve; the others are empirical effects, not curve accounts.
Lansdale & Baguley's fragmentation/trace account makes recall probability a function of the proportion of correct traces retrieved.
